citation.sourceDocument: Connecticut DRS Form CT-1040 Tax Calculation Schedule (2025), Tables A–E: brackets, exemption phase-out, recapture, personal credit (Conn. Gen. Stat. §12-700)
citation.sourceNote: Connecticut is the deepest single tax computation the engine models — the entire CT-1040 Tax Calculation Schedule (TCS), Tables A through E, every figure read verbatim from the official 2025 form (Rev. 12/25). Connecticut income tax = (Table B initial tax + Table C 2% phase-out add-back + Table D tax recapture) × (1 − Table E personal-credit decimal), on Connecticut taxable income = Connecticut AGI (≈ federal AGI for wage income) minus the Table A personal exemption. The exemption (Table A) is $15,000 single / $24,000 married jointly / $19,000 head of household and phases out DOLLAR-FOR-DOLLAR — reduced $1,000 for each $1,000 of AGI over $30,000 / $48,000 / $38,000, reaching $0 by $44,000 / $71,000 / $56,000 — modeled via the engine's standardDeductionPhaseOut (reductionRate 1.0, the personal exemption standing in for a standard deduction; the continuous ramp differs from the statute's $1,000-step 'or fraction thereof' by ≤$1,000 of exemption in the top $1,000 of each phase-out band). The seven-rate schedule (Table B) is 2% / 4.5% / 5.5% / 6% / 6.5% / 6.9% / 6.99% over per-status thresholds (single 4.5% over $10,000 up to 6.99% over $500,000; married jointly the same rates over doubled thresholds, top 6.99% over $1,000,000; head of household over $16,000 … $800,000). DISTINCTIVE — two stacked HIGH-INCOME RECAPTURES that claw back the benefit of the lower brackets so the top earners pay a near-flat 6.99%: Table C (the 2% Tax Rate Phase-Out Add-Back, max $250/$500/$400 single/joint/HoH) and Table D (the Tax Recapture, max $3,400/$6,800/$5,320), both functions of AGI. Because the exemption is fully phased out before any recapture begins (single recapture starts at AGI $56,500, the exemption is gone by $44,000), taxable income equals AGI throughout the recapture range — so both tables are modeled exactly on taxable income via the engine's per-status `incomeRecapture` stages (Table C plus Table D's three sub-ramps), with only a ≤ one-step linear-vs-step residual inside each ramp (the steps are $25–$90 single, larger at the highest incomes). The Table E PERSONAL TAX CREDIT — a credit of 0.75 down to 0 of the recapture-inclusive tax, sliding with AGI in published steps — is modeled exactly via the engine's new `personalCreditRate` step table per filing status (so a $60,000 single filer's 10% credit is applied, not dropped). Married filing separately maps to single (its true exemption $12,000, Table C band, and Table E steps differ slightly — a rare-status launch-fidelity approximation); a qualifying surviving spouse maps to married jointly. The 2025 figures are used for the 2026 shard (the latest official CT-1040 TCS; Connecticut's brackets and tables are statutory and were not changed for 2026), cross-checked against the Tax Foundation 2026 table. Connecticut allows no standard deduction beyond the personal exemption; the property-tax credit, the EITC, the pass-through-entity-tax credit, and other Connecticut credits are omitted at launch fidelity; no local income tax. Worked example, single $60,000 wages: AGI $60,000 > $44,000 so the exemption is $0 and Connecticut taxable income = $60,000; Table B = 2%·10,000 + 4.5%·40,000 + 5.5%·10,000 = $2,550; Table C (AGI $60,000, band $56,500–$61,500) ≈ $19.44 by the engine's ramp ($25 by the published step); Table E credit decimal = 0.10 (single, $33,300–$60,000); Connecticut income tax = (2,550 + 19.44) × (1 − 0.10) = $2,312.50. Verified against the official 2025 CT-1040 Tax Calculation Schedule (Tables A–E) and Conn. Gen. Stat. §12-700, and cross-checked against the Tax Foundation 2026 state-rate table on 2026-06-08.
